items.title: Understanding the Presentation and Impact of Gastrointestinal Bleeding (GIB)
items.content: Gastrointestinal bleeding can present as overt or occult. Overt GIB is identified by visible signs such as hematemesis, melena, or hematochezia, while occult GIB may manifest through symptoms related to anemia or a positive fecal occult blood test. GIB is the leading cause of gastrointestinal-related hospitalizations in the U.S., with significant morbidity and mortality primarily due to decompensation of underlying conditions rather than direct exsanguination.
keyPoints: Overt GIB presents as hematemesis, melena, or hematochezia.
Occult GIB may present with symptoms of anemia or a positive fecal occult blood test.
~530,000 hospital admissions annually in the U.S. due to GIB.
Case fatality rate is ~2%, primarily from decompensation of other illnesses.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · Gastrointestinal Bleeding

items.title: Categorizing and Understanding the Severity of Gastrointestinal Bleeding
items.content: Gastrointestinal bleeding is categorized by its site: upper (esophagus, stomach, duodenum), lower (colon), small intestinal, or obscure if the source is unclear. The condition is a major cause of hospital admissions in the U.S., with a significant case fatality rate due to complications from underlying illnesses rather than direct blood loss.
keyPoints: GIB categorized by site: upper, lower, small intestinal, or obscure.
~530,000 hospital admissions annually in the U.S. due to GIB.
Case fatality rate is ~2%, primarily from decompensation of other illnesses.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · Gastrointestinal Bleeding

items.title: Management of High-Risk Peptic Ulcers
items.content: High-risk peptic ulcers, characterized by active bleeding or a nonbleeding visible vessel, require urgent intervention. Endoscopic therapy significantly reduces further bleeding and associated complications.
keyPoints: Peptic ulcers account for ~50% of UGIB hospitalizations.
20% of patients with bleeding ulcers have high-risk findings (active bleeding or nonbleeding visible vessel).
Endoscopic therapies include bipolar electrocoagulation, heater probe, injection therapy, clips, and topical hemostatic powder.
High-dose PPIs after endoscopy reduce further bleeding and mortality in high-risk patients.
Patients with clean-based ulcers have near-zero rebleeding rates.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · SOURCES OF GASTROINTESTINAL BLEEDING (part 1)

items.title: Prevention of Recurrent Peptic Ulcer Bleeding
items.content: Recurrent bleeding in peptic ulcers is mitigated by addressing Helicobacter pylori, NSAIDs, and acid. Eradication of H. pylori significantly lowers rebleeding rates.
keyPoints: 10–50% of patients with bleeding ulcers may rebleed within a year without preventive measures.
H. pylori eradication reduces rebleeding to <5%.
NSAIDs should be discontinued or replaced with COX-2 selective NSAIDs and PPIs if necessary.
Aspirin for secondary prevention of cardiovascular disease should not be stopped, but can be held temporarily in UGIB cases.
Patients with ulcers unrelated to H. pylori or NSAIDs need indefinite PPI therapy due to a 42% rebleeding rate at 7 years.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · SOURCES OF GASTROINTESTINAL BLEEDING (part 1)

items.title: Management of Esophageal Varices
items.content: Esophageal variceal bleeding is managed with endoscopic ligation and vasoactive medications, with TIPS as an option for refractory cases.
keyPoints: Variceal hemorrhage accounts for 2–40% of UGIB hospitalizations, varying by population.
Endoscopic ligation combined with IV vasoactive medication (e.g., octreotide) is standard treatment.
Nonselective beta blockers plus endoscopic therapy reduce recurrent bleeding more effectively than either alone.
TIPS recommended for persistent or recurrent variceal bleeding and in certain cases of acute variceal bleeding with advanced liver disease.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · SOURCES OF GASTROINTESTINAL BLEEDING (part 1)

items.title: Prophylaxis for Stress-Related Gastric Mucosal Injury
items.content: In critically ill patients, prophylactic use of proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) is recommended to reduce the risk of clinically significant gastrointestinal bleeding (GIB). This approach is supported by meta-analysis showing that PPIs are more effective than no prophylaxis or H2-receptor antagonists in preventing GIB without affecting mortality or infection rates.
keyPoints: Prophylaxis with PPIs reduces clinically important GIB more effectively than no prophylaxis or H2-receptor antagonists.

PPI prophylaxis is suggested for critically ill patients at high risk (≥4%) of bleeding, defined by specific criteria including mechanical ventilation without enteral nutrition, portal hypertension, cirrhosis, platelets <50 × 10^9/L, international normalized ratio >1.5, or two of the following: mechanical ventilation with enteral nutrition, acute kidney injury, sepsis, or shock.
Meta-analysis indicates no impact on mortality or risk of infection (e.g., pneumonia, Clostridioides difficile) from PPI prophylaxis.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · SOURCES OF GASTROINTESTINAL BLEEDING (part 2)

items.title: Management of Small-Intestinal Vascular Ectasias
items.content: Small-intestinal vascular ectasias often require endoscopic therapy initially, but rebleeding is common. Thalidomide and octreotide long-acting release are suggested for medical management if further bleeding occurs after initial endoscopic treatment.
keyPoints: Endoscopic therapy is the initial treatment for small-intestinal vascular ectasias.
Rebleeding rates are ~45% over a mean follow-up of ~2 years.
Thalidomide and octreotide long-acting release are recommended for further management after rebleeding.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · SOURCES OF GASTROINTESTINAL BLEEDING (part 2)

items.title: Approach to Diverticular Bleeding
items.content: Diverticular bleeding is typically abrupt, painless, and can be massive. Most cases stop spontaneously, but endoscopic or transcatheter arterial embolization may be necessary for persistent bleeding. Surgical resection is recommended for refractory cases.
keyPoints: Diverticular bleeding stops spontaneously in ≥90% of patients.
Rebleeding on long-term follow-up is ~15% over 4–5 years, with higher rates reported from Asia.
Endoscopic therapy may decrease recurrent bleeding if the specific bleeding diverticulum is identified during colonoscopy.
Transcatheter arterial embolization prevents further bleeding in most patients when identified at angiography.
Segmental surgical resection is recommended for refractory diverticular bleeding.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · SOURCES OF GASTROINTESTINAL BLEEDING (part 2)

items.subtopic: INITIAL ASSESSMENT | DIFFERENTIATION OF UGIB FROM LGIB
items.title: Assessing Hemodynamic Stability in Gastrointestinal Bleeding
items.content: In patients with gastrointestinal bleeding (GIB), initial assessment should focus on heart rate and blood pressure to evaluate hemodynamic stability. Clinically significant bleeding is indicated by postural changes, tachycardia, and recumbent hypotension. Hemoglobin levels may not immediately reflect the severity of acute GIB due to proportionate reductions in plasma and red cell volumes.
keyPoints: Heart rate and blood pressure are primary indicators for initial assessment.
Hemodynamically significant bleeding causes postural changes, tachycardia, and recumbent hypotension.
Hemoglobin may be normal or minimally decreased initially in severe GIB due to 'whole blood' loss.
Transfusion is recommended when hemoglobin drops below 7 g/dL.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · INITIAL ASSESSMENT | DIFFERENTIATION OF UGIB FROM LGIB

items.subtopic: INITIAL ASSESSMENT | DIFFERENTIATION OF UGIB FROM LGIB
items.title: Differentiating Upper from Lower Gastrointestinal Bleeding
items.content: Hematemesis suggests an upper gastrointestinal bleeding (UGIB) source, while melena indicates blood has been in the GI tract for ≥14 hours. Hematochezia usually points to a lower GI source but can also indicate UGIB if associated with hemodynamic instability and dropping hemoglobin. Nonbloody nasogastric aspirates may be seen in ~15% of serious UGIB cases, and bile-stained aspirates do not exclude UGIB as they are incorrect in ~50% of cases.
keyPoints: Hematemesis indicates UGIB; melena suggests blood presence for ≥14 hours.
Hematochezia usually points to lower GI bleeding but can indicate UGIB with instability.
~15% of serious UGIB cases may have nonbloody nasogastric aspirates.
Bile-stained aspirate reports are incorrect in ~50% of UGIB cases.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · INITIAL ASSESSMENT | DIFFERENTIATION OF UGIB FROM LGIB

items.title: Discharge Criteria for Low-Risk UGIB Patients
items.content: Patients with a Glasgow-Blatchford score of 0–1 can be safely discharged from the emergency room, as they have a very low risk (~1%) of requiring transfusion, hemostatic intervention, or death.
keyPoints: Glasgow-Blatchford score range: 0–23
~1% risk for patients with scores 0–1 needing transfusion, intervention, or dying
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · EVALUATION AND MANAGEMENT OF UGIB (FIG. 51-1)

items.title: Timing and Impact of Endoscopy in UGIB
items.content: Upper endoscopy should be performed within 24 hours for most hospitalized UGIB patients, regardless of risk level. Urgent endoscopy within 6–12 hours does not improve outcomes and may increase mortality in high-risk patients.
keyPoints: Endoscopy recommended within 24 hours
No benefit from urgent endoscopy (6-12 hours) for high-risk patients; may increase mortality
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · EVALUATION AND MANAGEMENT OF UGIB (FIG. 51-1)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a manifestation of overt gastrointestinal bleeding (GIB)?
items.explanation: Iron-deficiency anemia is a manifestation of occult, not overt GIB.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · Gastrointestinal Bleeding

items.question: Which site is NOT categorized under upper gastrointestinal bleeding (GIB)?
items.explanation: The colon is categorized as a lower GIB site, not an upper one.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · Gastrointestinal Bleeding

items.question: What percentage of patients with gastrointestinal bleeding (GIB) die from exsanguination?
items.explanation: Patients generally die from decompensation of other illnesses, not exsanguination.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · Gastrointestinal Bleeding

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a recommended treatment for high-risk peptic ulcers?
options: High-dose proton pump inhibitors given as a constant infusion
Discontinuation of NSAIDs without further management
items.explanation: High-risk peptic ulcers are managed with endoscopic therapy and high-dose PPIs, not merely by discontinuing NSAIDs.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · SOURCES OF GASTROINTESTINAL BLEEDING (part 1)

items.question: What is the recommended management for a patient with bleeding esophageal varices?
options: Combination of endoscopic ligation and IV vasoactive medication
items.explanation: A combination of endoscopic ligation and IV vasoactive medication is superior to either therapy alone for esophageal varices.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · SOURCES OF GASTROINTESTINAL BLEEDING (part 1)

items.question: Which factor does NOT contribute to peptic ulcer pathogenesis?
items.explanation: Cyclooxygenase-2 selective NSAIDs are used to reduce the risk of ulcers, not a contributing factor in their pathogenesis.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · SOURCES OF GASTROINTESTINAL BLEEDING (part 1)

items.question: What is the typical outcome for patients with Mallory-Weiss tears?
options: Bleeding stops spontaneously in ~80–90% of patients
Endoscopic therapy is required in all cases
Mortality rate is high due to complications
items.explanation: Bleeding from Mallory-Weiss tears stops spontaneously in approximately 80–90% of patients.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · SOURCES OF GASTROINTESTINAL BLEEDING (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as a high-risk factor for bleeding in critically ill patients requiring PPI prophylaxis?
items.explanation: A hemoglobin level of >15 g/dL is not a high-risk factor for bleeding in critically ill patients.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · SOURCES OF GASTROINTESTINAL BLEEDING (part 2)

items.question: What is the most common cause of significant small-intestinal GIB in children?
items.explanation: Meckel's diverticulum is the most common cause of significant small-intestinal GIB in children.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · SOURCES OF GASTROINTESTINAL BLEEDING (part 2)

items.question: Which therapy has shown marked reductions in bleeding episodes, transfusions, and hospitalizations for small-intestinal vascular ectasias?
items.explanation: A multicenter double-blind randomized trial showed that thalidomide significantly reduces bleeding episodes, transfusions, and hospitalizations.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · SOURCES OF GASTROINTESTINAL BLEEDING (part 2)

items.question: What is the most common cause of lower gastrointestinal bleeding in adults after excluding local anal processes?
items.explanation: After excluding local anal processes, diverticulosis is the most common cause of lower gastrointestinal bleeding in adults.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · SOURCES OF GASTROINTESTINAL BLEEDING (part 2)

items.subtopic: INITIAL ASSESSMENT | DIFFERENTIATION OF UGIB FROM LGIB
items.question: Which clinical sign indicates a likely upper gastrointestinal bleeding (UGIB) source?
items.explanation: Melena indicates blood has been present in the gastrointestinal tract for a significant time, suggesting an upper source.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · INITIAL ASSESSMENT | DIFFERENTIATION OF UGIB FROM LGIB

items.subtopic: INITIAL ASSESSMENT | DIFFERENTIATION OF UGIB FROM LGIB
items.question: What is the recommended hemoglobin threshold for transfusion in acute UGIB based on recent studies?
items.explanation: Transfusion is recommended when the hemoglobin drops below 7 g/dL, as it decreases rebleeding and death.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · INITIAL ASSESSMENT | DIFFERENTIATION OF UGIB FROM LGIB

items.subtopic: INITIAL ASSESSMENT | DIFFERENTIATION OF UGIB FROM LGIB
items.question: What does a bile-stained nasogastric aspirate not necessarily indicate in patients with suspected UGIB?
items.explanation: A bile-stained appearance does not exclude UGIB because reports of bile in the aspirate are incorrect in ~50% of cases.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · INITIAL ASSESSMENT | DIFFERENTIATION OF UGIB FROM LGIB

items.question: Which baseline characteristic is NOT predictive of rebleeding and death in patients with upper gastrointestinal bleeding?
items.explanation: A low Glasgow-Blatchford score is associated with very low risk of rebleeding and death.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · EVALUATION AND MANAGEMENT OF UGIB (FIG. 51-1)

items.question: What is the suggested timing for performing upper endoscopy in most patients hospitalized with UGIB?
items.explanation: Upper endoscopy should be performed within 24 hours in most patients hospitalized with UGIB.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · EVALUATION AND MANAGEMENT OF UGIB (FIG. 51-1)

items.question: Which medication is suggested to improve visualization at endoscopy for patients presenting with UGIB?
items.explanation: The promotility agent erythromycin is suggested to improve visualization at endoscopy.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · EVALUATION AND MANAGEMENT OF UGIB (FIG. 51-1)

items.statement: Occult gastrointestinal bleeding may present with symptoms such as lightheadedness and syncope.
items.explanation: These are symptoms of blood loss associated with occult GIB.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · Gastrointestinal Bleeding

items.statement: Gastrointestinal bleeding is the least common gastrointestinal condition leading to hospitalization in the United States.
items.explanation: GIB is the most common GI condition leading to hospitalization.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · Gastrointestinal Bleeding

items.statement: Eradication of H. pylori decreases rebleeding rates to less than 5% in patients with bleeding ulcers.
items.explanation: H. pylori eradication significantly reduces the risk of rebleeding in these patients.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · SOURCES OF GASTROINTESTINAL BLEEDING (part 1)

items.statement: Patients with lower-risk peptic ulcers should receive high-dose PPI therapy.
items.explanation: Lower-risk patients do not require endoscopic therapy and are managed with standard doses of oral PPI.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · SOURCES OF GASTROINTESTINAL BLEEDING (part 1)

items.statement: Stress-related gastric mucosal injury typically results in severe bleeding without ulceration.
items.explanation: Severe bleeding from stress-related gastric mucosal injury should not develop unless ulceration occurs.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · SOURCES OF GASTROINTESTINAL BLEEDING (part 2)

items.statement: Diverticular bleeding often presents with pain and chronic symptoms.
items.explanation: Diverticular bleeding is usually abrupt in onset, usually painless, sometimes massive.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · SOURCES OF GASTROINTESTINAL BLEEDING (part 2)

items.subtopic: INITIAL ASSESSMENT | DIFFERENTIATION OF UGIB FROM LGIB
items.statement: Hemoglobin levels fall immediately with acute gastrointestinal bleeding.
items.explanation: Hemoglobin does not fall immediately due to proportionate reductions in plasma and red cell volumes.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · INITIAL ASSESSMENT | DIFFERENTIATION OF UGIB FROM LGIB

items.subtopic: INITIAL ASSESSMENT | DIFFERENTIATION OF UGIB FROM LGIB
items.statement: Hyperactive bowel sounds can be a clue to upper gastrointestinal bleeding.
items.explanation: Hyperactive bowel sounds are associated with UGIB.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · INITIAL ASSESSMENT | DIFFERENTIATION OF UGIB FROM LGIB

items.statement: Early endoscopy within 6–12 hours in high-risk patients improves clinical outcomes.
items.explanation: More urgent endoscopy does not improve clinical outcomes and may increase mortality.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · EVALUATION AND MANAGEMENT OF UGIB (FIG. 51-1)

items.statement: Antibiotics are recommended for cirrhotic patients presenting with UGIB to decrease bacterial infections.
items.explanation: Antibiotics in cirrhotic patients reduce bacterial infections, rebleeding, and mortality.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · EVALUATION AND MANAGEMENT OF UGIB (FIG. 51-1)

items.assertion: Patients taking low-dose aspirin for secondary prevention should not discontinue it if they develop a bleeding ulcer.
items.reason: Immediate reinstitution of aspirin is associated with lower mortality compared to not restarting.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why aspirin should be continued.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · SOURCES OF GASTROINTESTINAL BLEEDING (part 1)

items.assertion: Patients with bleeding ulcers unrelated to H. pylori or NSAIDs do not require PPI therapy.
items.reason: Rebleeding rates are high without protective therapy in these patients.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; such patients should remain on PPI therapy indefinitely due to high rebleeding risk.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · SOURCES OF GASTROINTESTINAL BLEEDING (part 1)

items.assertion: Endoscopic therapy may decrease recurrent diverticular bleeding.
items.reason: Case series suggest endoscopic therapy identifies the specific bleeding diverticulum, reducing recurrence.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · SOURCES OF GASTROINTESTINAL BLEEDING (part 2)

items.assertion: Transcatheter arterial embolization is effective in preventing rebleeding from colonic vascular ectasias.
items.reason: Rebleeding rates are lower for diverticular bleeding than nondiverticular LGIB after transcatheter arterial embolization.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why transcatheter arterial embolization is effective in preventing rebleeding from colonic vascular ectasias.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · SOURCES OF GASTROINTESTINAL BLEEDING (part 2)

items.subtopic: INITIAL ASSESSMENT | DIFFERENTIATION OF UGIB FROM LGIB
items.assertion: Hematochezia is always indicative of a lower gastrointestinal bleeding source.
items.reason: An upper GI lesion may briskly transit blood through the bowel before melena develops, presenting as hematochezia.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because an upper GI lesion can present with hematochezia.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · INITIAL ASSESSMENT | DIFFERENTIATION OF UGIB FROM LGIB

items.assertion: PPI infusion at presentation reduces the need for endoscopic therapy.
items.reason: It decreases high-risk ulcer stigmata such as active bleeding.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Gastrointestinal Bleeding · EVALUATION AND MANAGEMENT OF UGIB (FIG. 51-1)
